critique.summary: The article recounts the author's experience discovering a CPU design bug in the Xbox 360 related to a special prefetch instruction (xdcbt) that bypassed the L2 cache, causing memory coherency issues and crashes. The bug was exacerbated by speculative execution of this instruction due to branch prediction, similar in principle to the Meltdown and Spectre vulnerabilities.
mainPoints: The Xbox 360 CPU is a three-core PowerPC chip with a 1MB L2 cache shared among cores.
A new instruction, xdcbt, was introduced to prefetch data directly into the L1 cache, bypassing L2, improving performance but breaking memory coherency guarantees.
Using xdcbt incorrectly caused heap corruption crashes due to incoherent views of memory between cores.
Speculative execution of xdcbt instructions caused crashes even when the instruction was not actually executed, due to branch prediction.
The bug was fixed by avoiding use of xdcbt in shipped games and careful prefetching to not cross buffer boundaries.
The issue is analogous to modern speculative execution vulnerabilities like Meltdown and Spectre.
critique.truthfulness: The article is a first-person technical account by a credible former Xbox 360 CPU engineer and is consistent with known details about the Xbox 360 architecture and speculative execution vulnerabilities. The described behavior of the xdcbt instruction and its impact on cache coherency aligns with established principles of CPU cache design and speculative execution. The connection to Meltdown and Spectre is conceptually accurate as both involve speculative execution side effects. The article includes technical explanations and corroboration from other experts in comments, enhancing credibility.
considerations: The article is anecdotal and based on personal experience; it does not cite external sources or official documentation.
The impact of the bug on shipped games beyond the mentioned title is not detailed.
The article does not discuss potential hardware fixes or mitigations beyond software avoidance.
The broader implications for CPU design and security are touched on but not deeply analyzed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the Xbox 360 CPU design bug and its implications, with some comments noting the speculative execution behavior and historical context of IBM's role in consoles. Other comments provide related links or off-topic remarks.

comments.author: dev-ns8
comments.text: Wow... A speculative branch prediction path actually get's preemptively executed despite the branch outcome? No matter if the execution has side-affects??? That's quite amazing. Are modern CPUs doing speculative execution like this and just put extra safeguards around affects or do they just prefetch / decode instructions now-a-days?
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully highlights the speculative execution behavior discussed in the article and raises relevant questions about modern CPU design, showing a good understanding of the topic.

comments.author: chasil
comments.text:
It is interesting that IBM dominated this generation of consoles, and was vanquished in the next.

The high failure rates of the Xbox 360 did not help.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Xbox_360_technical_problems
comments.analysis: This comment provides useful historical context linking IBM's console involvement and the Xbox 360's technical issues, which is relevant and accurate based on known facts.
